items.q: What process moves carbon from the atmosphere into plants?
items.why: Students confuse the directions. Photosynthesis takes CO2 OUT of the atmosphere (plants build sugars); respiration RELEASES CO2. Both run simultaneously in plants, but only photosynthesis fixes new carbon.

items.q: Why is burning fossil fuels considered to disrupt the carbon cycle?
items.correct: It releases carbon stored for millions of years much faster than the cycle can absorb.
items.misconception: It creates new carbon that did not exist before.
items.why: Students think combustion creates carbon. It RELEASES previously stored carbon - the issue is rate. Geological storage took millions of years; we're releasing it in centuries, overwhelming natural sinks.

items.q: Why is atmospheric nitrogen unavailable to most plants directly?
items.correct: N2 has a strong triple bond that most organisms cannot break.
items.misconception: Because plants cannot reach high enough to access it.
items.why: Students miss the chemistry. Despite 78% of air being N2, the molecule's triple bond is energetically costly to break. Only specialised nitrogen-fixing bacteria (and lightning, industrial Haber process) can convert it to usable forms.

items.q: Which reservoir contains the most carbon globally?
items.why: It's Oceans (~38 000 Gt C). “Atmosphere (~870 Gt C)” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Average atmospheric CO₂ residence time is approximately:
items.why: Correct answer: 5 years. “100 years” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If oceans absorb 25% of 40 Gt CO₂ yr⁻¹ anthropogenic emissions, ocean uptake (Gt CO₂ yr⁻¹) is?
items.why: Exact answer: 10. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
